This psalm opens with raw urgency. "Make haste, O God, to deliver me!" The psalmist is not praying politely or patiently. He is desperate. His enemies are actively seeking his life—not merely opposing him, but hunting him—and he cannot afford to wait. What strikes us here is that this is not a prayer for patience or long-suffering. It is a demand for speed, a cry that God act now. The writer knows his own weakness: he is "poor and needy," vulnerable and exposed. He has nowhere else to turn. Notice that he calls God by two names—Elohim and Adonai—emphasizing both God's power to deliver and His lordship over the situation. This is not a tentative request but an appeal grounded in the conviction that God can and must intervene immediately.

Yet even in crisis, the psalmist's vision expands beyond himself. He prays not only for his own deliverance but for his enemies to be "ashamed and confounded," to retreat in humiliation. This might sound vengeful, but the text suggests something deeper: the psalmist wants his enemies' shame to serve a purpose. It will prove that God is real, that He defends those who trust in Him, that mockery of the righteous carries consequences. The mocking cry "Aha, aha!"—the taunt of those who think they have won—will become hollow. And then the psalm pivots to something even more important: "May all who seek You rejoice and be glad in You." The psalmist's deliverance is not ultimately about him. It is about vindicating God's character before all who trust in Him.

This tension—between the psalmist's desperate need and his concern for God's glory—reveals what true faith looks like under pressure. He does not ask God to spare him so he can have a comfortable life. He asks because his enemies mock God, because those who love God's salvation need to see His faithfulness demonstrated, because God's name matters more than his own safety. Yet he also brings his poverty and need directly before God without apology. There is no contradiction here. The psalmist understands that his deliverance would glorify God before all who are watching. His life and God's honor are inseparably linked. This is why he can plead with such intensity for speed: "O LORD, do not delay." He is not being selfish. He is asking God to act in a way that will strengthen the faith of all who seek Him.

When we face opposition or danger, we often split our prayers in two directions—either focusing entirely on our own relief or entirely on how our situation reflects on God. Psalm 70 shows us that true prayer integrates both. Bring your desperation to God without shame. Tell Him that you are poor and needy. Ask Him to act quickly. But do not stop there. Ask that your deliverance would vindicate His name and strengthen the faith of others. Ask that His enemies might see His power and repent, and that His people might see His faithfulness and rejoice. When you pray this way, you stop being the center of your own petition. God becomes the center. And paradoxically, that is when your own deepest need is most likely to be met.
